summary: After rolling out its Liquid Glass redesign more broadly to iOS users last month, Meta is now moving to take the revamped look to its Mac app. Here are the details. more…

title: 'Transformative' pancreatic cancer drug doubles survival time
summary: People with advanced pancreatic cancer taking an experimental daily pill lived nearly twice as long as those receiving chemotherapy infusions

title: A golden age of maths is dawning and mathematicians are freaking out
summary: Mathematicians are stunned at the progress AI is making in solving advanced problems, leaving some questioning whether there will still be room for humans

title: How human error became a weapon against large language models
summary: Alan Turing proposed a test for machine intelligence: could a computer convince a human it was human? We have begun conducting the same test on ourselves, writes Max Moser

title: Geoengineering can thicken Arctic sea ice, but for how long?
summary: Two companies are aiming to preserve Arctic ice by pumping water onto the sheet and letting it freeze, but only one of the trials found that this delayed melting in the summer

title: The best new science-fiction books of June 2026
summary: There is plenty of intriguing sci-fi on offer this month, whether it’s solar-powered cities from Adrian Tchaikovsky or a strange future from M. John Harrison

title: Aim high but don't shoot for the moon, mathematicians advise
summary: According to a mathematical model of how people weigh up different outcomes, the optimal strategy is to be ambitious, but not overly so

title: Pancreatic cancer halted by virus injection in three patients
summary: A cancer-killing virus has stopped pancreatic tumours from growing and spreading in three people in an initial safety trial, raising hopes that it may help to beat the deadly condition

title: Q-Day could destroy bitcoin – and our retirement savings
summary: Even if you’ve never bought any cryptocurrency, like columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan, your money may be affected by bitcoin’s fate – which is uncertain, as quantum computing advances are threatening to make the encryption protecting it useless

title: Glaciers in the 'roof of the world' have suddenly started melting
summary: Until recently, the Pamir mountains in central Asia have bucked the global melting trend, but in 2025, the region’s glaciers experienced a massive loss of ice due to extreme heat

title: Mathematical AI helps researchers crack 50-year-old problem
summary: After an AI from OpenAI found a trick to solve an 80-year-old conjecture from Paul Erdős, mathematicians have borrowed the same technique to solve another important problem

title: Start-ups are racing to revolutionise mathematics with AI
summary: AI start-ups with hundreds of millions of dollars in funding are hiring mathematicians and building AI systems that they hope will not only solve mathematics, but also build more intelligent AI

title: 3D-printed lymph nodes could widen access to CAR T-cell therapy
summary: The cost of CAR T-cell therapy means that the highly effective cancer treatment is unavailable in many parts of the world. But a new way of making these cells could dramatically drive down the cost

title: هل يفلت الذكاء الاصطناعي من المساءلة؟
summary: في وقت يتسارع فيه انتشار تقنيات الذكاء الاصطناعي على نطاق غير مسبوق، يتصاعد الجدل حول حدود التنظيم ودور الحكومات في كبح مخاطر هذه الثورة التقنية، وسط تحولات سياسية تعيد رسم ملامح الحوكمة الرقمية في الولايات المتحدة والعالم.

title: Our verdict on Luminous by Silvia Park: a fascinating take on robots
summary: The New Scientist Book Club read Silvia Park's near-future sci-fi novel Luminous in May, and had lots of good things to say (along with a few complaints)

title: The late Ian Watson's sci-fi The Embedding is intriguing – but dated
summary: Watson's death last month prompted sci-fi columnist Emily H. Wilson to read his acclaimed 1973 debut and find out what she'd been missing. She found it fascinating – but reflective of its time

title: Unsettling dance piece explores how AI is warping human relationships
summary: Inspired by Shannon Vallor's book The AI Mirror, this compelling piece looks at how we are being affected by our deepening interactions with tech

title: Embryos made without sperm or eggs reveal why many pregnancies fail
summary: Embryo organoids made from stem cells are enabling scientists to recreate early pregnancy in the lab, unlocking treatments for infertility, miscarriage and pre-eclampsia

title: Space storms could switch train signals and cause serious accidents
summary: Critical safety equipment in many train systems is vulnerable to disruption by space weather, which could lead to fatal accidents

title: Earliest use of anaesthetics uncovered in Chinese doctor’s tomb
summary: Residues on medical equipment reveal that physicians in China over 600 years ago used aconitine, a highly toxic plant chemical, to alleviate pain during surgical procedures

title: Will lab-grown sperm let infertile men have children of their own?
summary: Men who do not produce sperm can’t be helped by existing fertility treatments, but a start-up is now claiming it can grow their sperm in the lab. Columnist Michael Le Page suspects this technique will have to be combined with gene editing if it is to help many men

title: Why your brain needs plenty of “Aha!” moments
summary: In the age of AI, instant answers to our questions are readily available. But columnist Helen Thomson finds that continuing to encourage those delicious flashes of insight that come from your own thoughts may be beneficial both for your everyday life and your long-term brain health

title: Mercury may have gained all of its unexpected water in a single day
summary: Despite being the closest planet to the sun, Mercury has thick deposits of ice at its poles, and now we may understand the events that formed them over just one Mercurian day

title: Experimental mRNA vaccine may protect against multiple Ebola viruses
summary: Tests with rodents suggest an mRNA vaccine in development offers protection against three strains of Ebola virus, including the one behind the current crisis

title: Mathematicians stunned by AI's biggest breakthrough in mathematics yet
summary: Artificial intelligence built by OpenAI has cracked a decades-old conjecture by Paul Erdős, which mathematicians have hailed as a monumental moment for AI in mathematics

title: Women’s better memories may delay Alzheimer’s diagnosis by years
summary: Women appear cognitively normal for almost three years longer than men after their brains start to develop Alzheimer’s disease, making it harder to diagnose and preventing early treatment

title: Photos reveal unexpected details from the world's first atomic test
summary: Previously classified photos and documents show the scientific work that went into the world's first atomic test in 1945 – a test that, just weeks later, would see nuclear bombs dropped in Japan

title: Can we harness quantum effects to create a new kind of healthcare?
summary: Experiments hint that quantum mechanisms are vital to the machinery of life. Now researchers are exploring if these effects help to explain the success of an array of puzzling health treatments

title: The Selfish Gene at 50: Why Dawkins’s evolution classic still holds up
summary: When Richard Dawkins’s first blockbuster book was published half a century ago, few genes had ever been sequenced or studied in detail. Yet the book’s gene-centred view of evolution still has much to teach us in today’s genetic age

title: Solar farm on the ocean outperforms land-based solar in Taiwan
summary: A solar farm in a tidal bay has generated more electricity and profits than a nearby coastal solar farm, but challenges could arise as floating solar moves further offshore

title: Colossal claims an artificial eggshell will help it bring back the moa
summary: Colossal Biosciences, the company that says it resurrected the dire wolf, now says it has developed artificial eggshells so it can replicate the huge eggs of the moa. Independent experts say this isn't nearly enough to bring back these giant birds

title: The future of robot armies is here – and it’s not what you think
summary: Robots are becoming more a part of our lives every year, and worries about a robot army rising up have long plagued the technology. But columnist Annalee Newitz talks to nanobot researchers and finds out the real robot army could be a welcome solution to medical or environmental problems

title: Flotation tanks deployed to combat PTSD after devastating wildfires
summary: Maui in Hawaii experienced some of the worst wildfires in US history in 2023. Amid concerns of a PTSD epidemic, flotation tanks are being deployed to the island to help restore people's mental health

title: How I used psychology to come back from the worst year of my life
summary: Work, illness, divorce: life is riddled with stressors out of your control. But research is revealing new ways to cope with these challenges and find hope instead of despair

title: The 3 things you need to know about protein, according to an expert
summary: Why have so many people become fixated on protein? Donald Layman is one of the people behind the research showing the benefits of getting more protein in your diet, but he thinks things have gone too far and wants to set the record straight

title: The Ebola emergency shines a light on the urgent need for new vaccines
summary: A little-known strain of Ebola virus is behind an ongoing health emergency, prompting researchers to call for the acceleration of vaccine candidates against such infections

title: The ‘doomsday’ glacier’s giant ice shelf is about to break away
summary: The floating ice shelf of world’s widest glacier – Thwaites glacier in Antarctica – is detaching, with worrying implications for global sea-level rise

title: Why autism pioneer Uta Frith wants to dismantle the spectrum
summary: After a career spent grappling with the neural underpinnings of autism, Uta Frith is unwavering in her controversial call to scrap our current view of the condition and start again
